options.text: We can easily type 'draw a cat' and it works instantly
They produce completely random categories based on noise
They only ever generate grey squares

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of a standard, unconditional GAN?
exercises.explain: Standard GANs start with pure random noise, meaning they generate random images with no way for us to request a specific category, like a cat or a dog.

cards.title: The Random Artist
cards.body: Imagine a magic paintbrush that paints beautiful landscapes, but you have absolutely no say in what it draws. One minute it's a snowy mountain, the next it's a desert. How do we take the steering wheel and tell the AI exactly what we want to create?

Conditional GANs (cGANs) solve this by feeding an extra piece of information—like a label, text, or a sketch—into both the Generator and the Discriminator. This 'condition' acts as a guide, directing the generation process toward a specific target.
cards.title: What is Conditioning?
The Restaurant Order
cards.body: Think of a standard GAN as a chef cooking a random dish. A Conditional GAN is like giving the chef an order ticket that says 'Spaghetti'. Now, the chef must make pasta, and the critic reviews it specifically as a pasta dish.

If we want to generate handwritten digits, we don't just feed the Generator random numbers. We also feed it the number '7'. The Generator learns to output an image of a 7, and the Discriminator checks if it is a realistic 7.
cards.title: Generating a Specific Digit

cards.body: You might think adding conditions makes the network perfectly accurate instantly. In reality, the GAN still has to learn how to connect the condition label to the visual features, which takes time and training!
cards.title: Does it work instantly?
Random vs. Controlled

left.text: Generates a random face or object from noise with no user control.

right.text: Generates a specific category (like 'smiling face') requested by the user.

cards.body: Compare how adding a condition changes the behavior of a GAN from random to directed.

exercises.explain: A conditional GAN takes an extra input (a condition like a label or sketch) to guide both the Generator and Discriminator.

options.text: It limits the GAN to only generating black and white images.
It guides the GAN by adding specific label or target information to the inputs.
It speeds up training by skipping the Discriminator.

exercises.prompt: How does conditioning change the input of a GAN?

exercises.explain: Both the Generator and the Discriminator receive the condition so they can align on what is being generated and evaluated.
exercises.prompt: True or False: In a conditional GAN, both the Generator and the Discriminator receive the condition input.

exercises.explain: By adding a condition (like a label or sketch), we can control what the GAN generates.
exercises.sentence: To guide a GAN to generate a specific class of image, we feed it a ______ alongside the random noise.

exercises.explain: AI Image Translation uses a sketch or source image as a condition to generate a fully detailed matching output image.
exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes a conditional generative task?

options.text: Generating a realistic color photo of a handbag directly from a simple hand-drawn sketch outline.
Taking a photo and saving it in a different file format like PNG or JPEG.
It allows us to translate structured inputs, like sketches or maps, into realistic photos.
It makes the GAN run faster on mobile devices.
It removes the need for a Discriminator entirely.

exercises.explain: Conditioning is the foundational step that lets us translate one image type to another (like sketches to photos). Next up: Pix2Pix.
exercises.prompt: Why is conditioning crucial for image-to-image translation?

concept: Conditioning shifts GANs from random generation to controllable generation, laying the groundwork for image translation. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Pix2Pix.'
title: From Randomness to Control
